event: The Book of Jonah — Jonah in distress
symbols: huddled figure — Jonah's despair and resistance to God's call
chaotic lines — the storm and inner turmoil of the reluctant prophet
large creature form at right — the great fish that swallowed Jonah
description: An expressionistic ink and wash drawing showing a hunched, distressed human figure at the left, rendered in aggressive black lines and dark washes. The figure appears seated or crouching with head bowed. At the right, a larger abstract form suggests an animal or sea creature. The background shows loose horizontal lines suggesting a landscape or seascape. The style is gestural, rapid, and emotionally charged, with stark black ink on white paper.
theological_description: The Book of Jonah is one of the Old Testament's most psychologically penetrating narratives. Jonah, called to preach repentance to Nineveh, flees from God's commission — only to discover that there is no escaping the divine will. The great fish that swallows him becomes paradoxically the instrument of his salvation: in the belly of the beast, Jonah prays and is delivered (Jonah 2). This expressionistic rendering captures the raw emotional turmoil of the prophet's experience — the terror of the storm, the darkness of the deep, the anguish of a man wrestling with God. Jesus Himself identified Jonah's three days in the fish as the sign prefiguring His own death and resurrection (Matthew 12:39-40). Jonah's story is ultimately about the impossibility of running from God and the scandalous breadth of divine mercy that extends even to Israel's enemies.

scripture_refs.reason: Jonah fleeing from God, the storm, and being swallowed by the great fish
Jonah's prayer from the belly of the fish — distress turning to deliverance
The sign of Jonah — three days in the fish prefiguring Christ's three days in the tomb
